Who Worte This:  Brown, Dan. The Da Vinci Code. Doubleday, 2003.
The novel "The Da Vinci Code" was written by Dan Brown and first published in 2003 by Doubleday.

What are their qualifications? When was it created?
When and where was it published? Who is publishing it?

Dan Brown, the author of "The Da Vinci Code," has a Bachelor of Arts degree in English from Amherst College and is a graduate of the Phillips Exeter Academy. He is a fiction author who has written several best-selling books including "Angels & Demons" and "The Lost Symbol."

"The Da Vinci Code" was first published on March 18, 2003, by Doubleday, a publishing company that is a division of Penguin Random House. It is a work of fiction that explores the fictional conspiracy theory involving the Holy Grail and the alleged marriage of Jesus Christ and Mary Magdalene.

The book has been published worldwide in multiple languages and has gone on to become a best-selling novel with millions of copies sold globally. It has been adapted into a feature film, stage play, and a video game.

Why was it created?
Why did the author choose this format?
Who is the audience?

The novel "The Da Vinci Code" was created as a work of fiction intended to entertain and intrigue readers with its thrilling plot and intricate conspiracy theories surrounding the Holy Grail and the alleged marriage of Jesus Christ and Mary Magdalene. The author, Dan Brown, has stated that he wrote the book as a way to explore the questions and possibilities raised by these conspiracy theories.

The author likely chose the novel format for "The Da Vinci Code" because it is a popular and effective way to tell a story that can engage and captivate readers. Novels allow for a lot of creative freedom and provide an expansive canvas for complex plots and characters.

The audience for "The Da Vinci Code" is mainly adult readers who enjoy mystery, suspense, and historical fiction. However, due to its popularity, the novel has also been read by a wide range of readers, including young adults. Its themes, such as conspiracy and secret societies, have made it appealing to readers who enjoy thrilling and intriguing narratives.
